message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Early CoVID-19 growth often obeys:
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20143149v1_inline1.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  , with
                  <jats:italic>K</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  = [(ln 2)/(
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  )], where
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  is the pandemic
                  <jats:italic>doubling time</jats:italic>
                  , prior to society-wide
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  . Previously, we modeled
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  with
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>dbl</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  as a linear function of time, where
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ]
                  <jats:bold>1</jats:bold>
                  ≈ exp[+
                  <jats:italic>K</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>A</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  / (1+,
                  <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  )] is used here. Additional parameters besides {
                  <jats:italic>K</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  } are needed to better model different
                  <jats:italic>ρ</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] =
                  <jats:italic>dN</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ]/
                  <jats:italic>dt</jats:italic>
                  shapes. Thus, a new
                  <jats:italic>Orthogonal Function Model</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>OFM</jats:italic>
                  ] is developed here using these orthogonal function series:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn1">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20143149v1_ueqn1.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  where
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                  ) and
                  <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] form an implicit
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ]
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ]) function, giving:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn2">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20143149v1_ueqn2.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  with
                  <jats:italic>L</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>m</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                  ) being the
                  <jats:italic>Laguerre Polynomials</jats:italic>
                  . At large
                  <jats:italic>M</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  values, nearly
                  <jats:italic>arbitrary</jats:italic>
                  functions for
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] and
                  <jats:italic>ρ</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] =
                  <jats:italic>dN</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ]/
                  <jats:italic>dt</jats:italic>
                  can be accommodated. How to determine {
                  <jats:italic>K</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>A</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>o</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  } and the {
                  <jats:italic>g</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>m</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  ;
                  <jats:italic>m</jats:italic>
                  = (0, +
                  <jats:italic>M</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  )} constants from any given
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>Z</jats:italic>
                  ) dataset is derived, with
                  <jats:italic>ρ</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] set by:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn3">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20143149v1_ueqn3.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  The
                  <jats:italic>bing com</jats:italic>
                  USA CoVID-19 data was analyzed using
                  <jats:italic>M</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  = (0, 1, 2) in the
                  <jats:italic>OFM</jats:italic>
                  . All results agreed to within about 10 percent, showing model robustness. Averaging over all these predictions gives the following overall estimates for the number of USA CoVID-19 cases at the pandemic end:
                  <jats:disp-formula id="ueqn4">
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20143149v1_ueqn4.gif" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:disp-formula>
                  which compares the pre- and post-early May
                  <jats:italic>bing com</jats:italic>
                  revisions. The CoVID-19 pandemic in Italy was examined next. The
                  <jats:italic>M</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  = 2 limit was inadequate to model the Italy
                  <jats:italic>ρ</jats:italic>
                  [
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ] pandemic tail. Thus, regions with a quick CoVID-19 pandemic shutoff may have additional
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  factors operating, beyond what can be easily modeled by just progressively lengthening pandemic
                  <jats:italic>doubling times</jats:italic>
                  (with
                  <jats:italic>13 Figures</jats:italic>
                  ).
                </jats:p>
title: Orthogonal Functions for Evaluating Social Distancing Impact on CoVID-19 Spread

reference.unstructured: https://www.MedRxiv.org/ID=MedRxiv/2020/043752v1, 03.25.2020, “Forecasting COVID-19 impact on hospital bed-days, ICU-days, ventilator-days and deaths by US state in the next 4 months”,
IHME COVID-19 Health Service Utilization Forecasting Team.
https://www.MedRxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.05.04.20091207v1, https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.05.04.20091207, “Initial Model for the Impact of Social Distancing on CoVID-19 Spread “, Genghmun Eng
“Univ. of Washington researchers predict 80,000 COVID-19 deaths in U.S. by July”, Alan Boyle, GeekWire, March 26, 2020.
U.S. death toll and lengthen state-by-state recovery timeline”, Alan Boyle, GeekWire, April 27, 2020.
“New pandemic projection puts U.S. death toll at nearly 135,000, due to less social distancing”, Alan Boyle, GeekWire, May 4, 2020.
“COVID-19: What7s New for May 4, 2020: Updated IHME COVID-19 projections: Predicting the Next Phase of the Epidemic”,
IHME COVID-19 Health Service Utilization Forecasting Team.

reference.article-title: Note of the Polynomials of Hermite and Laguerre
reference.journal-title: Journal of the London Mathematical Society
